data.title: Generic Drugs May Not Face Tariffs
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here are the latest trending news:

Path to peace: Israel and Hamas reached a ceasefire and hostage release deal in the first step towards ending the war in Gaza. Meanwhile, oil futures were largely flat.

More curbs: China has tightened controls on rare earth exports, its key leverage in trade talks with the U.S., ahead of a Trump-Xi meeting later this month.

ACA gridlock: President Trump said he's not willing to entertain talks about extending Affordable Care Act subsidies until the U.S. government reopens.

Possible exclusion

The Trump administration isn't planning to impose tariffs on generic drug imports, The Wall Street Journal reported, although the move isn't final and could change in the coming weeks. The exclusion from tariffs would bring some relief for generic drugmakers, as nine out of ten prescriptions filled in the U.S. are for generic drugs.

"The administration is not actively discussing imposing Section 232 tariffs against generic pharmaceuticals," said Kush Desai, White House deputy press secretary. A Commerce Department spokesperson said the ongoing 232 investigation into pharmaceuticals wouldn't result in tariffs on generic drugs. Section 232 empowers the U.S. president to impose tariffs on imports if they threaten national security.

Potential impact: Some officials reportedly cautioned against generic drug tariffs, saying the levies would result in price hikes and drug shortages. Tariffs are also unlikely to make U.S. generic drug production profitable, given the very low cost of producing them in countries like India. But some Commerce Department officials argued that the tariffs may be necessary to reduce U.S. dependence on global supply chains. There was no "daylight or disagreement between the Commerce Department and White House on this issue," spokesperson Desai said.

Reshore manufacturing: Desai said the administration is "implementing a nuanced and multi-faceted approach to onshore manufacturing of generic pharmaceuticals and ensure that Americans are never again left in the lurch due to foreign dependence as they were during the COVID era." Some officials are said to be looking into possible government assistance to reshore U.S. generic drugmaking. This could include federal grants or loans to U.S. manufacturers of generic drugs, potentially involving funds from foreign countries that were secured as part of tariff deals.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Why I'm Betting 75% Of My Portfolio On America's Future

Energy Transfer: It Is Time To Buy Units

AT&T: A Switch To Verizon Has Never Looked More Irresistible

3 Values In An Overbought Market

Bargain Prices On Credit Assets: The 'BDC Flu' Hits FSK, BBDC And NCDL

What else is happening...

Nvidia CEO confirms xAI investment, downplays AI bubble fears.

GM (GM) won't claim tax credit, but will fund its own EV incentive.

Akero Therapeutics (AKRO) surges on Novo (NVO) takeover news.

First Brands bankruptcy: These firms face exposure, potential risks.

Chevron (CVX) CEO signals Hess deal will surpass financial targets.

Most Fed members see further policy easing ahead - FOMC minutes.

CBO estimates: U.S. budget deficit narrows slightly to $1.8T in FY25.

IRS furloughs almost half its workforce as federal shutdown drags on.

Fiserv (FI), Bank of North Dakota tie up to launch state's first stablecoin.

Here's how Big Tobacco's shift to alternative nicotine products is going.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +1.8%. Hong Kong -0.3%. China +1.3%. India +0.5%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.3%. Paris +0.3%. Frankfurt +0.3%.
Futures at 7:00, Dow flat. S&P flat. Nasdaq -0.1%. Crude -0.6% to $62.20. Gold -0.2% to $4,061.30. Bitcoin -0.5% to $122,341.
Ten-year Treasury Yield unchanged at 4.12%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include PepsiCo (PEP) and Tilray Brands (TLRY).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: 'Go Your Own Way,' Sing Lagarde And Powell
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski & James Knightley

Remember when former ECB President Jean-Claude Trichet coined the phrase, 'a very strong brotherhood of mutual admiration?' He was talking about his fellow central bankers. Don't be fooled that this remains a cosy club. Mutual respect may well be there. And while there's a lazy assumption that the European Central Bank simply follows the Fed, it really has become something of an urban legend. True, until the financial crisis, it did look a bit like that. But between 2014 and 2020, central banks went their separate ways. And we're seeing that again right now; it's a separation that looks set to continue.

While the ECB has made itself comfortable in a ‘good place,’ with inflation settling down at around 2% and growth expected between 1% and 1.5%, the Fed has just restarted its rate-cutting cycle. So, we've got monetary policy inactivity in the eurozone and rate cuts in the US. If anything, we have the US Federal Reserve following the ECB, not the other way around.

Why we're looking at 4 more Fed rate cuts

Below the surface of a strong US economy in the second quarter – inflation at 3% and stock markets at all-time highs – risks to the Fed’s dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment are shifting. Recent payrolls numbers and subsequent downward revisions led the Fed to acknowledge that the jobs market no longer looks "solid." The government shutdown is restricting official data flows, but private sector indicators suggest that the 'low hire, low fire' economy could become a “no hire, let's fire” market.

At the same time, the inflationary threat from tariffs has not materialised as quickly as feared. The average tariff rate is estimated to be around 18% based on announced country and sector rates, but collected customs revenues imply a realised rate closer to 10%. Companies are seemingly absorbing this cost increase for now. This provides more time for disinflationary pressures from falling energy costs, weakening housing rents and slowing wage costs to offset the tariff impact.

A weaker jobs backdrop, coupled with lower-than-expected inflation, justifies moving interest rates down towards neutral and leaves us forecasting October and December interest rate cuts, with a further two 25bp cuts in the first half of next year.

Don't rule out another ECB cut

Contrary to the Fed, the ECB has already brought interest rates towards neutral. And while ECB officials want to make markets believe that the rate cut cycle is over, we still see a chance of at least one more rate cut. If the ECB’s own rather benign forecasts for growth and inflation turn out to be too optimistic, the ECB could be forced to fine-tune its monetary policy stance. In such a scenario, roles between the two brothers (or sisters), leader and follower, would be switched again.

But in our base case scenario, we see the Fed and the ECB dancing to the tune of the old Fleetwood Mac song 'Go Your Own Way.' Something they have been doing much more often than many markets think.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cummins: Watch This Dividend Aristocrat In The Making Now
data.description:
Dividends flowing into a brokerage account evoke wonderful feelings. At least for me, anyways.

When I see the dividends stack up, it reminds me that I'm on the path to achieving financial independence (passive income > expenses). While I'm already doing what I love for a living, reaching FI will be a huge milestone for me.

In the dividend growth investing game, there's another event that's just as likely to spike my dopamine levels, though: a position announcing a big dividend hike.

This recently happened with Cummins (NYSE:CMI). In July, the company boosted its quarterly dividend per share by 9.9% to $2.00. This came in much better than my expectation of a 7.1% increase in the quarterly dividend per share to $1.95.

When I last covered Cummins with a hold rating in January, I was optimistic about its start of full production of its X15N natural gas engine at its Jamestown Engine plant. I also liked its financial prowess. The only downside was that shares looked expensive at the time.

Fast forward to today, and I'm reiterating my hold rating. Cummins' latest product launch reinforces my upbeat opinion on the company's fundamentals. As does the A-rated balance sheet. The disconnect here is that shares have rallied more than I believe is warranted by its respectable fundamentals.

Innovation and Long-term Trends Can Power Growth for Cummins

On August 5th, Cummins released its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's revenue decreased by 1.7% year-over-year to $8.64 billion in the quarter. For more color, this surpassed Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $180 million.

What was behind this slight revenue decline for the second quarter? The company's Engine and Components segments experienced weakness. Tariff uncertainty led to weaker demand in on-highway truck markets. That resulted in a 6% decrease in revenue in the U.S. and Canada.

As a result, the company's Engine segment recorded $2.90 billion in revenue in the second quarter. This was down 8% over the year-ago period. Cummins' Components segment fell by 9.3% year-over-year to $2.71 billion during the quarter.

The company isn't sitting around and just taking these headwinds on the chin, however. As SA analyst Grassroots Trading put it in a recent article, Cummins is limiting the damage by leaning into faster-growing markets with impressive demand.

The chief growth catalyst for Cummins for the second quarter was a record performance from its Power Systems segment. Revenue spiked 18.9% higher in the quarter to $1.89 billion. During the Q2 2025 Earnings Call, CFO Mark Smith attributed this performance to robust demand in data center applications/other mission-critical applications and favorable pricing.

The secondary growth tailwind for Cummins during the second quarter was record performance in the Distribution segment. The business posted $3.04 billion for the quarter, which was up 7.5% over the year-ago period.

Cummins' diluted EPS soared 22.2% year-over-year to $6.43 in the second quarter. What makes this surge in diluted EPS all the more impressive is that it was $1.27 above the analyst consensus during the quarter. Improved operating efficiency and higher Power Generation demand resulted in a 200 basis point improvement in the net profit margin to 10.3% for the quarter. That's how diluted EPS grew at a double-digit percentage clip, while revenue marginally decreased.

Looking ahead, I'm optimistic about Cummins' future.

For one, two of the company's four largest segments are firing on all cylinders. As an eventual ruling comes from the Supreme Court on President Trump's tariffs, the uncertainty holding back its other two key segments could finally be lifted. That could be enough to return the company to strong growth.

Not to mention that growth looks like it can be largely sustained in Cummins' Power Systems' segment. This is because the company recently launched its newest 17-liter engine platform generator. According to CEO and Chairwoman Jennifer Rumsey, the S17 Centum genset produces up to 1 megawatt of power. Its compact design is aimed at meeting growing power needs in urban environments, where compact design and high performance are mission-critical.

On a more macro level, the segment also has undeniable growth catalysts. The market research firm Precedence Research projects that the data center generator market will compound by 7.3% annually from $9.2 billion in 2025 to $17.3 billion by 2034.

Thus, I believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for diluted EPS over the next few years is realistic. In 2025, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for diluted EPS to grow by 2.5% to $22.02. For 2026, an additional 12.8% upswing to $24.84 is anticipated. In 2027, another 17.4% jump in diluted EPS to $29.16 is being predicted.

Cummins is on a firm footing financially, too. In the first half of 2025, the company's interest coverage ratio was 14.4. That's why Cummins possesses an A credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to Cummins Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release, Cummins Q2 2025 10-Q Filing, and Cummins Q2 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value is Around $350 a Share

Since my previous article, Cummins' shares have roared 20% higher. That significantly outpaced the 12% total return logged by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

This has pushed the forward 12-month P/E ratio from an already elevated level of 16.8 in January to 17.9 as of now. For more context, that's well above the 10-year average P/E ratio of 14.4, according to FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, I believe that a reasonable fair value P/E ratio for Cummins is 14.4. Why do I think that the stock deserves such a multiple?

Well, the long-term FactSet annual diluted EPS growth consensus is 9.1%. This is in line with the 10-year average annual diluted EPS growth rate of around 9%, which suggests that Cummins' growth potential is completely intact. That should continue to support a fair value P/E ratio near 15.

As things stand, the calendar year 2025 is 77% behind us. This implies another 23% of 2025 and 77% of 2026 still lie ahead over the coming 12 months. That produces a forward 12-month diluted EPS input of $24.18.

Applying my fair value P/E ratio of 14.4, I compute a fair value of $349 a share. Relative to the current $433 share price (as of October 7th, 2025), this works out to a 24% premium to fair value.

If Cummins matches the growth consensus and returns to fair value, it would deliver a 6% annual total return by the end of 2030 from current levels. This is why I'm waiting for a correction down to a forward 12-month P/E ratio of around 14 (around $340 a share).

Alternatively, the share price could stay relatively range-bound from here and grow into the multiple over the next 12 to 18 months. At such a point, Cummins would have a much more likely path to low-double-digit annual total returns over the next several years.

Cummins Can Keep Revving the Dividend Growth Engine

Cummins' 1.8% forward dividend yield remains greater than the industrial sector median forward yield of 1.5%. Since January, this spread has narrowed by about 20 basis points. That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System issues B and C+ grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (the latter metric is down from a B- grade in January).

Cummins's higher starting income isn't the only thing it has going for it: the company has upped its dividend for 19 straight years. This is already much better than the sector median of 3 years and puts Cummins on pace to become a Dividend Aristocrat in 2031. That's enough to earn an A+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency.

Cummins looks primed to build on this dividend growth streak as well. For 2025, the diluted EPS payout ratio is set to be in the mid-30% range. That provides a meaningful cushion for the company to withstand a temporary decline in its profits that is typical in economic downcycles. This is why the Quant System awards an A+ grade to Cummins for overall dividend safety, too.

That dividend sustainability also supports a healthy growth outlook for the payout. The Quant System anticipates a 7.5% annual forward dividend per share growth rate for Cummins. This would be nearly 100 basis points above the sector median forecast of 6.6%.

Given the manageable payout ratio for Cummins and solid growth prospects, I would even contend that this estimate is a bit too low. At any rate, Cummins is assigned a B- grade from the Quant System for forward dividend per share growth (unchanged from January). It also receives a B+ grade for overall dividend growth.

Risks to Consider

Cummins' fundamentals point to a company with plenty of growth on the horizon. However, some risks could potentially hurt the investment thesis if they play out.

The first risk to Cummins is that it's likely to face tariff uncertainty-related headwinds. During its Q2 2025 Earnings Call, the company noted that it anticipates North America heavy- and medium-term truck volumes to drop by 25% to 30% over the year-ago period in Q3 2025.

Any prolonged or worsening tariff uncertainty could lead the company to underperform expectations in the quarters ahead. This could pressure the company's fundamentals, leading to downward analyst revenue and diluted EPS revisions.

Similarly, Cummins is economically sensitive. With Kalshi's betting odds for a recession in 2025 down to just 4%, a recession doesn't seem imminent. Yet, it's still worth noting that whenever the next economic downturn happens, Cummins' diluted EPS growth could materially decelerate or even go negative for a year or two.

In my view, these types of events could be what trigger the stock's next selloff. From the current valuation, the selloff could potentially be enough to land it in a bear market. This is precisely what weighs on the stock's risk-reward dynamic.

Another risk to Cummins is linked to its heavy unionization. As of December 31st, 2024, almost one-third of its global workforce was represented by various trade unions. These were under collective bargaining agreements that expire between 2025 and 2029 (page 15 of 162 of Cummins' 10-K Filing).

If Cummins can't negotiate new contracts with these labor unions before they expire, the company's operations and supply chains could be disrupted. Even if the company reaches agreements before these contracts expire, satisfying the demands of the labor unions could require big pay raises or enhanced benefits packages for employees. Without improved employee productivity, that could crimp Cummins' profit margins.

Summary: Buy Cummins on the Next Correction

Cummins is an industry leader that's not resting on its laurels. The company's recent innovation and mega-growth trends, like data centers, promise to be catalysts for the company in the years ahead. The A-rated balance sheet is another indicator of vigorous fundamentals.

However, these fundamentals already seem to be more than priced into the stock. For me to add to my position and upgrade the stock back to a buy rating, I'd need to see a selloff, the company grow its way into the valuation, or a combination of the two. In the meantime, I'm happy to hold my 1.1% weighting in Cummins, making it my portfolio's 29th-biggest holding.

data.title: Tenet Healthcare: USPI Momentum Expected To Sustain Q3 Earnings, Why I Choose To Buy
data.description:
Tenet Healthcare Corporation (NYSE:THC) stock is flying so high at levels near both 52-week and all-time highs. These levels were last achieved in September 2002, over two decades ago. This also is in light of the announcement of the report date for Q3 2025 results on October 28, later this month. In terms of fundamentals, the company has been strategically shifting toward high-margin outpatient care while managing its high-acuity hospitals. The company's growth trajectory, despite facing significant reimbursement and litigation risks, to me supports a BUY rating. Despite trading at all-time high levels, I still believe it might continue this trend given the low valuation so far.

Tenet Healthcare is a Dallas-headquartered diversified healthcare services company. The company operates the largest ambulatory platform, USPI, alongside a national network of acute care hospitals and its Conifer revenue cycle management subsidiary.

Understanding Tenet's Business Model

Tenet Healthcare is in the business of running hospitals and outpatient surgery centres. What this means is that it owns and manages hospitals to ensure success and profitability.

Its revenue is earned each time a patient is treated or a surgery is performed, mainly through payments from private insurers, Medicare and Medicaid.

The company has two main strategic business units (SBUs) which are large hospitals that handle emergencies and complex surgeries, and a growing network of outpatient centres that perform same-day procedures. Tenet's outpatient side runs under the name United Surgical Partners International (USPI). This unit has proved to be more profitable and cheaper to run, and the company has been investing heavily there.

The company also runs a billing arm under the name Conifer. This section helps hospitals in collecting money faster and more accurately.

Overall, Tenet's strategy focuses on high-cost hospital care, expand outpatient centres, control labour and supply costs, and also using technology to improve efficiency. After earning cash, the company then employs it to buy back shares, pay down debt, and acquire new centres.

There is a general trend that has seen more surgeries moving outside hospitals. The company's big opportunity is that it owns both settings, thereby allowing it to benefit either way.

Assessing Revenue Drivers

In Q2 2025, the company recorded $5.27 billion in net operating revenue and $1.12 billion in adjusted EBITDA. This was a reflection of strong momentum across its two main segments.

Hospital Operations and Services was the main revenue contributor at $4.0 billion, representing about 76% of total revenue. This revenue line was driven by higher-acuity cases and improved payer mix. Same-hospital revenue per adjusted admission was up 5.2%.

On the other hand, ambulatory care (USPI) generated about $1.1 billion, which was around 21% of revenue. The unit delivered 7.7% same-facility growth and 39% EBITDA margins after benefiting from expanding outpatient surgical volumes and new facility openings. Meanwhile, Conifer, the revenue-cycle unit, provided the remaining 3% supported by automation and efficiency gains.

Looking ahead, the company is seeing revenue growth being underpinned by new USPI centres, high-acuity hospital programs, and AI-enhanced billing systems. Tenet's goal here is to shift its earnings mix toward higher-margin, tech-enabled, and cost-efficient outpatient care over the next three years.

Assessing Tenet's Growth Engines

USPI

USPI division is the company's key growth engine. This unit is benefiting from the rapid shift of surgeries from hospitals to outpatient centres.

Before trying to predict the future trajectory of this division, I think it's important to look at the progress made so far. One of the major advantages is that Tenet is the largest U.S. ambulatory surgery operator and has over 480 facilities.

During Q2, as highlighted above, USPI delivered 7.7% same-facility revenue growth and 8.3% higher revenue per case. The unit also added seven new centres and one surgical hospital during the quarter. The company also allocates around $250 million annually to acquire or build new ambulatory surgery centres (ASCs). Interestingly, the company then focuses on high-demand specialties like orthopaedics and spine. Meanwhile, total joint procedures rose 12.6% during Q2.

I view this growth as credible and sustainable given the consistency this unit delivered over the past quarters, averaging mid- to high single-digit organic growth with new centres being added.

Additionally, regulatory changes by Centres for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) expanding outpatient-eligible procedures and insurer incentives for lower-cost settings further validate the ongoing migration of complex surgeries to ASCs.

Premium Care Focus

Interestingly, Tenet's units do complement each other. Hospital growth is driven by expanding high-acuity programs in cardiac, orthopaedic, neurosurgery, and trauma care. These services do earn higher reimbursements per case. Also of interest is that the company has been investing in trauma and surgical capacity in major markets. In the process, Tenet has been divesting lower-margin hospitals.

Conifer

Tenet's Conifer division, although being the least revenue contributor, has seen modest revenue growth in Q2 while EBITDA margins improved. The improvements were lifted by robotic process automation that reduced claim denials and sped up collections.

On top of that, management reported a record improvement in days sales outstanding (DSO), showing faster cash recovery. The company's ongoing investments in predictive analytics are enhancing claim success and forecasting accuracy. In my view, these measurable gains confirm that AI-driven automation is already strengthening Tenet’s working capital and operational efficiency.

Key Risks Faced by Tenet

The company's main risks are clearly evidenced in its recent results and disclosures, and the biggest one is reimbursement risk.

This risk is so because about 40% of hospital revenue comes from Medicare, Medicaid, and supplemental Medicaid payments. This means that any form of cuts, delays, or rule changes in these programs can quickly reduce the company's top line. For instance, in Q2 2025, Tenet booked a US$79 million favourable prior-period Medicaid adjustment. Despite the development having been a positive one, the same can happen in the opposite direction, clearly showing how dependent earnings are on variable state funding.

Operationally, I also think that Tenet carries some risks. Its hospitals have high fixed costs, making the company vulnerable to volume shifts. In Q2, inpatient admissions rose just 1.6%, and to me that means that small declines can pressure margins.

Also, labour costs have remained sensitive. Despite the fact that Tenet has cut contract labour since 2023, wage inflation has the potential to erode the 25% year-over-year hospital EBITDA growth achieved in Q2.

The company's financial risk comes from leverage and aggressive buybacks. Over US$1.1 billion shares have been repurchased so far in 2025. I think that this could limit flexibility if cash flow weakens. Execution risk can also be faced in trying to integrate USPI’s eight new facilities while sustaining its margins at around 39%. I think that will require precise execution.

Litigation and Compliance Exposure

I thought I would look at this risk separately. Tenet is faced with a regulatory and legal battle against its St. Vincent Hospital. This legal issue is still ongoing, and the downside of this battle could be so bad for the bad.

Amongst the company's multiple actions is a major CMS/DPH investigation that found the hospital in "immediate jeopardy" due to dangerous understaffing. This issue was linked to at least three patient deaths. The most significant financial threat is the potential termination of all Medicare and/or Medicaid funding. Most noteworthy, this funding accounts for over 70% of revenue. This was threatened by March 2025 and remains a risk due to the hospital's alleged failure to fully implement corrective actions.

In addition, Tenet is also facing whistleblower lawsuits from nurses. This includes one filed by Carla LeBlanc, seeking significant damages and reinstatement. Given Tenet’s history of large settlements, a resolution for the lawsuits and regulatory fines is a strong possibility in my view and will likely occur over the next year or two.

In Q2, the company incurred a litigation and investigation expense of $28 million. If the ongoing cases happen to turn south for the company, this cost line could jump even higher. Also, any tempering of the Medicare fund could have very damaging effects on the company's top line.

Recent Policy Related Changes

In terms of policy, Tenet is being affected by some potential changes. The good thing is that most of the changes will affect it in the positive.

CMS's 2026 Proposed Rule includes changes like a three-year phase-out of the "Inpatient-Only (IPO) list" to shift surgeries to outpatient/ASC settings plus an expansion of the ASC Covered Procedures List by around 276 procedures. That said, a modest 2.4% payment rate increase for ASCs has been proposed.

Meanwhile, the 2025 Final Rule finalized a 2.9% net payment increase. Additionally, from 2025-2027, non-opioid pain treatments will receive separate payments in ASC/outpatient settings.

Potential Medicaid financing restrictions via the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" could reduce state provider payment boosts.

For Tenet, IPO elimination favours their high-margin USPI ambulatory business, rate increases offer minor inflation relief, and Medicaid changes pose a risk to hospital revenue.

Overall, I'm of the view that these policy shifts largely favour Tenet. I think the expanded outpatient surgery list and higher ASC payment rates should boost USPI growth and margins. However, tighter Medicaid financing rules could slightly pressure hospital revenue by reducing supplemental state payments.

Key Focus Areas Ahead of Q3 Results

Heading into the Q3 2025 results on October 28, I expect Tenet to deliver another solid quarter. However, I anticipate that the tone may shift from major upside surprises to evidence of stability and discipline.

In my view, USPI will again be the standout and likely to sustain the mid-to-high single-digit same-facility growth as new centres and higher-acuity outpatient procedures continue to lift revenue. Hospital margins should remain healthy and this should be supported by mix improvements and cost control. Overall admissions growth will likely stay modest.

I also think the company is going to record ongoing efficiency gains from AI automation at Conifer and we might see further improvement in collections and denial rates. Labour costs may increase marginally but remain manageable, I think. Reduced reliance on contract staff will likely help in containing labour costs.

I don't foresee the St. Vincent litigation having an immediate financial impact this month. That said, policy tailwinds like expanded outpatient eligibility should reinforce the growth narrative. Overall, I anticipate steady operational execution, reaffirmed guidance, and cautious optimism heading into 2026.

Valuation

Tenet Healthcare's valuation appears attractive compared to both the industry average and its main competitor, HCA Healthcare. Tenet's forward EV/EBITDA of 7.33 is significantly lower than the industry average of 12.36 and HCA Health's 9.96. I believe that this suggests that the market is valuing Tenet's future earnings power at a discount. Given its strong growth engine in the high-margin USPI division and continuous efficiency gains at Conifer, the lower multiple could indicate that Tenet is undervalued. However, the discount may also reflect the significant reimbursement and litigation risks highlighted, particularly the potential threat to Medicare/Medicaid funding at St. Vincent Hospital.

Conclusion

I view Tenet as a company with a successful strategy given its shift toward the high-growth and high-margin USPI segment. This is being coupled with favourable policy tailwinds like the expansion of outpatient-eligible procedures, its financial trajectory looks strong and sustainable. Meanwhile, the forward EV/EBITDA of 7.33 offers a significant discount to the industry average, which appears to over-reflect the known risks, including leverage and the St. Vincent litigation. The company’s consistent organic growth in its USPI engine suggests the risk/reward profile is favourable. Based on the compelling valuation and clear focus on accretive growth, I assign a BUY rating on THC stock.

data.title: Forget Ares Capital: I Am Buying Kayne Anderson BDC On The Dip Instead
data.description:
The BDC sector has pulled back sharply recently, with the sector down about 12% over the past month based on the performance of the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD). Ares Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:ARCC) and Kayne Anderson BDC (NYSE:KBDC) are no exception, as their shares have fallen by nearly as much over that same period.

While ARCC is a highly reputable BDC with a long-term track record of delivering phenomenal total returns:

I am buying newcomer KBDC on the dip instead of ARCC, and in this article, I will detail why.

Why Portfolio Defense Matters

The first reason why I am buying KBDC instead of ARCC is because KBDC’s portfolio is more defensively positioned than ARCC's is. ARCC has 59% of its portfolio invested in first-lien senior secured loans and a total of 64% invested in first- and second-lien senior secured loans combined. Meanwhile, KBDC’s portfolio is 98% invested in first-lien senior secured loans, giving it a much more conservative capital stack seniority profile and stronger downside protection in the event of an economic slowdown.

This is important because the market is becoming increasingly concerned about a weakening economy, as both the labor market and consumer sentiment have been declining based on recent economic data. This could impact BDCs (BIZD) by potentially leading to a rise in non-accruals and, as a result, a decline in net investment income and net asset value per share. With KBDC’s more defensively positioned investment portfolio, it stands to better weather this kind of environment than ARCC may, as it has greater equity exposure that will likely suffer greater losses than senior secured loans in a weakening economic environment.

Better Balance Sheet For A Higher-For-Longer World

The second reason why I like KBDC more than ARCC right now is that its balance sheet is better positioned to handle higher-for-longer long-term interest rates. I personally believe that long-term interest rates will remain elevated for an extended period, given that inflation continues to run persistently above the Federal Reserve’s target and the U.S. government continues to run an enormous budget deficit, putting ongoing pressure on the bond market.

KBDC is better positioned than ARCC for this scenario because, while both have low leverage ratios, KBDC’s is lower at 0.91x compared to ARCC’s 1.01x debt-to-equity ratio. KBDC also faces no major refinancing headwinds, as its nearest maturing debt issuances are not due until 2027 and 2028. Both of those maturities are fixed-rate notes at 8.65% and 8.74%, respectively. When those notes eventually come due, the company will likely be able to refinance them at materially lower interest rates given how high the interest rate on them is currently, turning what would normally be a headwind right now for BDCs into a tailwind for KBDC's NII per share.

Meanwhile, the remainder of KBDC’s debt (which is the vast majority of it) is floating-rate. This means that as the Federal Reserve cuts rates, KBDC’s borrowing costs will decline as well, directly improving profitability by helping to offset some of the headwinds to NII that the rate cuts will have on the yields on its loan investments.

ARCC, by contrast, has fixed-rate debt maturing in the near future, including notes that mature in January of next year at a 3.875% rate, notes maturing in July of next year at 2.15%, and additional notes maturing in June of 2027 and 2028 at 2.875% each. All of these debt issuances will almost certainly need to be refinanced at much higher interest rates than they are currently charging, creating a significant headwind to ARCC’s bottom line in the process.

Valuation: KBDC’s Advantage Over ARCC

The third reason why I like KBDC more than ARCC right now is due to the significant gap in their current valuation metrics. While ARCC’s strong long-term track record does justify a valuation premium to some extent, that premium is offset by the fact that ARCC charges among the highest management fees of any publicly traded BDC, whereas KBDC has some of the lowest fees among externally managed BDCs.

At the moment, ARCC trades at a slight premium to its net asset value, whereas KBDC trades at a steep 16% discount to its net asset value. Given that ARCC charges materially higher fees than KBDC, has a portfolio that is less defensively positioned to weather a potential economic downturn, and faces greater balance sheet headwinds with less incremental debt capacity, it makes little sense that ARCC would be trading at such a large valuation premium to KBDC.

KBDC, by contrast, has significantly more room to increase net investment income per share to offset potential headwinds from Federal Reserve rate cuts or weakening portfolio performance if the economy slows. When you combine these factors, KBDC’s portfolio structure, balance sheet positioning, and valuation all look much more attractive than ARCC’s right now. Additionally, KBDC’s base dividend yield of roughly 11.8% is very appealing, especially when compared to ARCC’s dividend yield of about 9.7%, as it offers investors a 210 basis point yield premium backed by a more defensive portfolio, stronger balance sheet, and much greater valuation margin of safety on a price-to-NAV basis.

Investor Takeaway

As a result of these three big reasons to favor KBDC over ARCC, the choice is obvious for me. Sure, ARCC is a great BDC that some would even call the best of breed. I agree that at the right price, ARCC is a buy. Moreover, its experience and proven management skill at selecting investments earn it some slack when comparing it to peers. However, the gap in valuation has gotten too large between ARCC and BDCs like KBDC when also factoring in KBDC's lower fees and strong balance sheet and portfolio capital stack positioning. Therefore, while ARCC's recent pullback has made it appear much more attractively priced than it was previously, and a further pullback could push it into buy range, for now I am steering clear of ARCC in favor of more attractively priced BDCs like KBDC in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: U.S. Tariffs Persist Beyond The Headlines
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

Beyond the daily churn of breaking news, US tariffs remain firmly in place. In fact, over the past month, the US government has reaffirmed its commitment to using them as an important policy instrument.

US President Donald Trump announced a new round of tariffs at the end of September. These new tariffs range from 25% on heavy trucks and 50% on kitchen cabinets and bathroom vanities to 100% on pharmaceuticals. These tariffs came under Section 232, declaring products as a threat to national security. And as little as kitchen cabinets are a threat to US national security as European washing machines were during Donald Trump's first term in office, these tariffs should be considered a contingency plan, ready to be activated if the US Supreme Court blocks the government's bilateral tariffs in its final ruling on 5 November.

On a more positive note, the US government also announced that it had acknowledged the EU’s start of the legislative process, outlined in the trade framework agreement between the EU and the US, which in turn triggers a retroactive reduction of US tariffs on European automobiles to 15%, from the current rate of 27.5%.

All of this shows that the shifts in global trade will continue, and it will take longer than expected before the full impact on the global economy will unfold. Currently, for example, the effective US tariff rate implemented by US customs is only some 10%, while based on the trade agreements known so far, it should be close to 20%.

As regards our base-case assumption for trade and tariffs, we stick to the belief of an average US tariff rate of close to 20% until the end of Trump’s full term in office. Tariffs remain one of Trump’s main instruments in foreign policy, but also an important source of public finance funding.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Aegon: a.s.r. Sell-Down Boosts Flexibility; Valuation Still Attractive
data.description:
We revisit Aegon (NYSE:AEG) (OTCPK:AEGOF) following its H1 results and, more importantly, September’s sale of 12.5 million a.s.r. shares — a development not yet flagged by Seeking Alpha's analysts. While our stance on EU banks is still upbeat, the current peak-like valuation backdrop makes this transaction attractive. In our latest update, we were positive on Aegon based on 1) a new share repurchase announcement, 2) positive read-across from the Corebridge VA deal, supporting Aegon's annuities segment, 3) solid fundamentals with Operating Capital Generation, and 4) upside from the Transamerica segment. From a share-price perspective, the company continues to deliver, and in numbers, since early July 2025, Aegon is up by almost 12.5% (including the dividend payment - Fig. 1).

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Upside

As usual, starting with the quarterly comment, Aegon's operating capital generation was aligned with Wall Street analyst expectations; however, it included some positive one-time items. At ceteris paribus, a few negative one-offs hit the P&L bottom line; the quarter looks balanced. In numbers, H1 operating results reached €845 million, up 19% compared to H1 2024. These positive results also reached the free cash flow generation, which increased by 18% to €442 million. Reported net profit reached €606 million, with a negative offset of other charges (-€183 million in the TLB Universal Life book and in US Variable Annuities) and positive fair value items (+€154 million in the UK). CSM was a drag, but was negatively impacted by the FX development. All in all, the company demonstrated the usual solid fundamentals, with solid capital positions. Indeed, both RBC and solvency ratios are ahead of the respective regulatory requirements.

There was no change in 2025 guidance; however, the company (pre-Q2) announced a €200 million share buyback, taking the total share buyback to €400 million, to be completed by mid-December (this represents 3.5% of the current market cap). This also did not account for the a.s.r.'s latest transaction.

Source: Aegon Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still Positive?

  1. Aegon sold 12.5 million a.s.r. shares via an accelerated bookbuild, trimming its stake from 29.96% to approximately 24%. It remains a committed anchor shareholder with unchanged governance rights and has locked up the remaining equity stake for 180 days. At September’s price, the divestment equates to roughly €725 million (about 7% of Aegon’s market capitalization). a.s.r. participated in the placement, repurchasing up to 15% of the shares sold. Proceeds will be allocated under Aegon’s capital framework. This will support new value-accretive opportunities, de-leveraging, and potential returns to shareholders. While the divestment could reduce Group FCF by €50 million from 2026 (approximately 6% of total, considering the dividend generation), it also enables at least €250 million of net debt reduction. Ultimately, we view the transaction as constructive for both companies, and it enhances Aegon’s financial flexibility;
  2. Once again, on a positive read-across from the latest Swiss Re release, we see Aegon benefiting from the evolution of the lower mortality rate. Swiss Re estimates that broad adoption of GLP-1 therapies for obesity could lower overall mortality by up to 6.4% in the US and 5.1% in the UK by 2045. The reductions are 4.0% and 3.2% in its base case, respectively. Under that base case, according to the Fiscal Year 2024 report sensitivity analysis, Aegon would see a positive net earnings impact from a 4% improvement in U.S. mortality, translating to approximately €150 million of cumulative net benefit over time;
  3. We see upside in the company's latest "Strategic Developments." In the press release, Aegon reported "Review announced on relocating Aegon’s legal domicile and head office to the United States." The US re-domicile will make possible an NYSE listing as a primary listing alongside Euronext. The company is already progressing with US GAAP, and we expect higher visibility at the Capital Market Day on 10th December. We believe a potential relocation will likely occur in 2-3 years. We view this move favourably for multiple re-ratings and higher liquidity.

Valuation

With guidance unchanged and our latest July update, we see little reason to alter our financial forecasts or valuation framework; the above developments reinforce our Buy rating. Our profit forecast of €1.25 billion is also aligned with the Aegon income range. This represents an EPS of €0.75 based on the current outstanding shares. The company also disclosed its valuation equity and the contractual service margin post-tax. The company communicated a value per share of €8.47 (Fig. 3). While this is the company's book value, it implies a discount of almost 20%. In our valuation, continuing to apply an unchanged 10x P/E multiple, Aegon's value reached €7.5 per share ($8.75 in ADR). This does not account for the 2026 forecast and the ongoing buyback.

Fig 3

Risks

We have covered Aegon well over these years, so downside risks include: 1) higher mortality rate, 2) higher competition in key markets, 3) higher regulatory risks (with the need to deploy more capital as a buffer to market shocks), 4) sharp reduction in public markets (both in the equity and fixed income), and 5) FX changes. Additionally, Aegon is exposed to banking risks via a.s.r. investment.

Conclusion

We believe the company has the correct fundamentals to continue to deliver. We see the a.s.r. transaction and the July share buyback announcement positively. With higher repurchase activities ahead and M&A upside, Aegon remains a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: High Upside As Revenue Keeps Accelerating At 12x P/E
data.description:
For the past month, news headlines have followed faithfully as OpenAI CEO Sam Altman globetrots to secure capacity from the chip giants to support ChatGPT's ravenous appetite for compute. News of a recent deal is what drove shares of AMD up ~40% this week alone.

My take here: while compute is certainly the star in running AI inferences, don't forget the storage chips that are also necessary in building out giant AI data centers. This is where Micron (NASDAQ:MU) is the undisputed star: the Boise, Idaho-based chipmaker has been buoyed by accelerating demand, particularly for its high-performance DRAM chips. Finally out of its cyclical penalty box, shares of Micron have soared ~2x this year: and yet, the stock still remains quite undervalued.

I last wrote a buy article on Micron in June, when the stock was still trading at ~$130 per share. Since then, Micron has been one of the strongest performers in my portfolio, surging ~50%. And yet despite these gains, I'm still holding on faithfully to my position, especially as the company has issued a robust outlook for demand. Especially with the company noting that supply dynamics are tight and likely to keep pushing prices higher, I'm reiterating my buy rating on Micron.

As a reminder for investors who are relatively newer to Micron, the company is currently enjoying substantial growth tailwinds in each of its core end-markets that are giving Micron a multi-year inflection:

  • Data centers: Now well over half of Micron's revenue, it's needless to say that the ravenous appetite for data center buildouts has lifted all of Micron's product lines. The company is expecting mid-single-digit growth in server chips, a business it had originally thought would post flat performance. Meanwhile, the company's HBM (high bandwidth memory) chips have reached an ~$8 billion annualized run rate, with demand for production secured in long-term contracts through FY26.
  • PCs: It's not all about data centers, either. Micron expects to benefit from a PC sales uplift, driven in particular by customers upgrading older PCs to AI-capable notebooks. Also, the end of support for Windows 10 in October is expected to drive a rush of upgraders for those holding onto older machines.
  • Smartphones: Meanwhile, in the mobile space, the lowest ends of each smartphone model are now increasing, with 128GB now the standard low-end capacity: a virtual necessity, given the increased resolution of pictures and videos in cameras of continually improving quality. This has buoyed Micron's NAND product lines.
  • Auto: Tesla (TSLA) and other automakers continue to push forward with a vision for fully-autonomous cars, which are requiring higher memory and storage needs.

The point here is to illustrate that investors typically associate Micron with cyclical demand, similar to how car companies and airlines typically trade at lower P/E ratios because investors expect revenue and EPS to ebb and flow. The memory industry has often gone through boom and bust periods of strong demand, suppliers like Micron racing to boost production, leading to oversupply and margin erosion. But with all of Micron's end-markets and end-products experiencing multi-year demand tailwinds, this is unlikely to happen this time.

Stay long here and keep riding Micron's rally higher.

Q4 Download

Let's now dig into more specific quantitative evidence for the aforementioned strength in Micron's markets. The fiscal Q4 (August quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Micron's revenue soared 46% y/y to $11.31 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $11.16 billion (+44% y/y) by a two-point margin. Revenue growth also accelerated nine points versus Q3's 37% y/y growth rate, which offers even more compelling evidence that Micron is at the beginning of a demand inflection.

Here, the company has had plenty of support from rising memory prices. The snapshot below details Micron's performance commentary by each of its two key memory products, DRAM and NAND. Key to note is that sequentially versus Q3, ASPs for both DRAM and NAND increased, by a low double digit and high single-digit percentage, respectively.

Rising DRAM prices aren't "new news", as demand for data centers has been fueling DRAM prices over the past year. But the turnaround in NAND pricing is relatively new: as recently as Q3, NAND pricing had softened. We note here that SanDisk, which is the other major player in NAND supply, appears to be following through on its commitment to maintain supply discipline after the company spun off from Western Digital, which is now focused on HDD chips.

Even more important is that Micron is expecting the benign pricing environment to continue well into FY26. The company's market outlook expects both DRAM and NAND demand to grow in the teens, with both product segments now expecting stronger demand than prior expectations.

The company is expecting supply growth in the industry to trail behind demand growth, which is a strong signal for continued price inflation for both DRAM and NAND. We note that we place high confidence in Micron's outlook, especially because in its most consequential product segment (HBM), the company has already secured customer commitments for nearly the entirety of FY26 supply. Per CEO Sanjay Mehrotra's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In addition to traditional servers, AI server growth continues to be very robust. This growth in both traditional and AI servers is driving strong demand for our DRAM products [...]

Our HBM customer base has expanded and now includes six customers. We have pricing agreements with almost all customers for a vast majority of our HBM3E supply in calendar 2026. We are in active discussions with customers on the specifications and volumes for HBM4, and we expect to conclude agreements to sell out the remainder of our total HBM calendar 2026 supply in the coming months."

On the profitability front, strong unit shipments and healthy pricing dynamics have pushed Micron's gross margins to 44.7%, which is an 800bps sequential improvement versus Q3 and approximately 10 points of y/y expansion.

And specifically for Q1, as shown above, the company is expecting "price, cost, and mix" to all support a continued rise in gross margins, to a forecasted 50.5-52.5% range in Q1.

Micron's pro forma EPS of $3.03 multiplied by 4x on a y/y basis, and beat Wall Street's expectations of $2.86 with 5% upside. Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting Micron to deliver 43% revenue growth in FY26, with full-year EPS roughly doubling to $16.63 (bearing in mind that Micron will enjoy the optical benefit of a 53-week fiscal year in FY26).

Valuation And Key Takeaways

Meanwhile, one of the underlying reasons why I remain stoutly bullish on Micron is that, considering the pace of the company's earnings expansion, Micron still remains very reasonably valued relative to other semiconductor stock peers.

As shown above, Micron's ~12x forward P/E sits substantially below flashier peers like AMD and Nvidia (NVDA), despite the fact of Micron's soaring growth that is buoyed by the same secular AI tailwinds. And even when we compare it versus other legacy peers like Seagate (STX) and Western Digital (WDC), Micron still trades at a substantial discount, which is why I view Micron to be the safest buy of the group.

Stay long here and keep riding this rally higher.

data.title: The USA Could Easily Spend Billions On AI
data.description:
By Jan Frederik Slijkerman & Timothy Rahill

Capital expenditures by data centres

As has been well documented, the large US technology companies are accelerating their data centre investments on the back of strong expected demand for new, AI-based services. Expected growth for these new services compounds already strong growth for the Cloud services segment. As can be seen below, investments by the largest technology companies are expected to surpass the US$400bn mark in 2026.

US big tech on an investment spree (US$bn)

These investments are enabled by strong cash generation

The investments described above are mind-blowing, given their scale. What is even more striking is that these investments have been funded from operating cash flows. In contrast, Europe lacks large, listed peers to Microsoft, Alphabet, or Amazon, making such self-funded investment less feasible.

As there is no such software company generating billions of free cash flow, there is also not a natural investor investing in the next growth phase of the cloud data centre business. This is a pity, because Europe is also not home to the biggest venture capital firms and listed asset managers.

US technology platforms fund their expenditures from their cash flows (US$m)

Could accelerating data centre investments pose risks to credit markets?

There are many uncertainties with respect to the required investments as well as the revenue opportunities coming from AI services. From a debtholder perspective, we are less concerned with a potential mismatch between supply and demand, as the large technology platforms mentioned above have funded their expansion plans from their cash flows.

Besides these large technology platforms, there are also pure-play data centres that have issued euro-denominated debt. The business model of these data centres benefits from a critical hub function. That is not to say that every AI and data centre start-up will be successful. However, as venture capital-backed companies typically do not issue investment-grade debt, we see limited risks for our market.

On the contrary, we think data centre investments in Europe are lagging global developments.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: JSI: Plodding Along, But With A Hick-Up (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF (JSI) at the beginning of the year, when we assigned the fixed-income exchange-traded fund a 'Buy' rating. In our original article, the first one to cover this name on the Seeking Alpha platform, we highlighted the robust fund composition and its low duration profile. The name is up over 3.3% since, but has suffered a recent 'hick-up,' which we are going to discuss.

An ABS fund

JSI is an ETF that focuses on the various sectors in the securitization market:

  • ABS: 39%
  • CMBS: 28.5%
  • Non-Agency Mortgages: 24.5%
  • Agency Mortgages: 15.5%
  • CLO: 3.6%
  • Other: residual

As per its objective statement, the ETF focuses solely on securitizations:

For investors looking for income diversification and higher yield potential. JSI aims to generate income with lower drawdown risk by actively allocating to high-quality opportunities across the U.S. securitized market.

One has to keep in mind, however, that in order to get a higher yield, the ETF invests in both investment grade and sub investment grade securities:

  • AAA: 22.1%
  • AA: 22.6%
  • A: 15.7%
  • BBB: 25.9%
  • BB: 12.4%
  • B: 4%
  • CCC: 0.3%

The below-investment-grade bucket is small at roughly 17%, but it can pose issues, and we shall see that shortly.

Recent price 'hick-up'

If we look at JSI's price performance, we will notice an interesting occurrence:

The fund's price does not move around much, with a -2.5% drawdown in April 2025, and another -1.5% drawdown now in September 2025. The recent occurrence is peculiar because the market has been in risk-on mode and rallying. So what happened?

Securitizations come with risks

On September 10, 2025 Tricolor Auto announced it was going out of business, and more peculiarly, it would go into liquidation rather than Chapter 11 reorganization:

Tricolor Holdings, a used car seller and subprime lender that focuses on undocumented immigrants in the US Southwest, filed to liquidate in bankruptcy. US prosecutors are also looking into allegations of fraud by the company. Tricolor’s Chapter 7 bankruptcy petition, filed in US Bankruptcy Court in Dallas, claimed liabilities between $1 billion and $10 billion and assets in the same range. A Chapter 7 liquidation is typically used by companies unable to fix themselves by slashing debt or otherwise restructuring and are instead selling assets through a court-supervised process.

If we look at the price action in JSI in the past month, we can see the correlation:

The fund started a large drawdown (for this name) once Tricolor announced its liquidation. But why would an ETF that holds securitizations care about a corporate bankruptcy? The reason lies in the holdings:

The fund holds several tranches of Tricolor securitizations, and the price move on those securities was violent when the corporation defaulted. For example, the first security is the C-tranche from the 2025-1A securitization, and as per market color, the C-tranche moved from par to mid 30s pricing after the bankruptcy.

Well why would securitizations go down in value when the corporate collapses, aren't securitizations supposed to be bankruptcy remote? Indeed they are, but there are tweaks. Firstly, when the original issuer and servicer go bust, a securitization will see its loan default ratio increase and its recoveries decrease. That is because the company that was supposed to do that is no longer there, and even a backup servicer will not be able to do the same job as fast and as good.

But in this case there is a much larger issue at play:

The public space is now showing data that fraud might be involved here, with the underlying loans pledged twice, and the issuing banks for the securitizations not doing their work in terms of checking the integrity of the underlying collateral. While nobody knows as of now if that is truly the case, the market started pricing the Tricolor securitizations for the worst case scenario, hence the substantial drop in price for JSI.

The takeaway for a retail investor here is that securitizations are not without risk, and when the overall credit markets are too complacent, one can get very nasty 'surprises' in terms of certain names. It is the portfolio manager's duty and responsibility to choose credits from good issuers that do not have gap to default risks like Tricolor. We are not experts in the subprime securitization market, but we can bet our bottom dollar that Tricolor was not one of the 'mint' issuer names in that space. JSI went for additional yield and experienced a gap-down in price.

Yield considerations and analytics

Let us remind ourselves of the yields JSI produces, and its analytics:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 5.84%
  • 1-year Total Return: 5.77%
  • Duration: 2.5 years
  • Expense ratio: 0.49%
  • AUM: $1.2 billion

Before the Tricolor debacle, JSI was producing a very attractive 1-year total return in excess of 6.8%. Now it is back to being a simple carry vehicle.

The macro is becoming cloudy

Currently credit conditions are very benign, with ample liquidity and very tight spreads. Yet we get instances like Tricolor. One does not have to do a lot of math to realize that in a true recession the underlying holdings here can experience significant stress. Both market stress (temporary) and actual defaults (permanent). We are now switching our view for JSI based on overall macro to account for the increased gap-down risk that Tricolor brought to light.

Market risk will also become more pervasive with the potential for -5% to -6% drawdowns in the fund if liquidity disappears and we have an increase in defaults in the overall credit universe. And also keep in mind that bankruptcies for ABS issuers usually result in lower recoveries for their securitizations and higher default rates. This all happens during times of stress in credit.

Also to keep in mind that many of the underlying securities in JSI are floating rate, which means that their all-in yields will decrease with Fed Funds. Given the market is pricing 100 bps of cuts in the next 12 months, the net yield for the ETF is set to decrease.

Conclusion

JSI is a fixed-income ETF. The name focuses on securitizations and has a 17% allocation to sub-investment grade names. The ETF has experienced a -1.5% drawdown on the back of the Tricolor company bankruptcy, event that highlights the hazards of underwriting higher-risk names in the subprime auto space. With a cloudy macro and lower Fed Funds on the horizon, we are downgrading this fund to 'Hold.'
